explanation: The statement 'The autopilot shall prop_0 when prop_1 and prop_2' translates to 'Globally, if prop_1 and prop_2 are true, then prop_0 must also be true'. This is a universal implication.

The statement "The autopilot shall prop_0 when the prop_1 and prop_2" implies a global implication. It means that whenever `prop_1` and `prop_2` are true, `prop_0` must also be true. This is expressed as `G((prop_1 & prop_2) -> prop_0)`.

The statement "The autopilot shall prop_0 when prop_1 and prop_2" implies a global condition. It means that at all times, if 'prop_1' and 'prop_2' are true, then 'prop_0' must also be true. This translates directly to a global implication: G((prop_1 & prop_2) -> prop_0).

The statement "The autopilot shall prop_0 when prop_1 and prop_2" implies a global requirement. Whenever 'prop_1' and 'prop_2' are simultaneously true, 'prop_0' must also be true. This is a global implication: G((prop_1 & prop_2) -> prop_0).

The statement "The autopilot shall prop_0 when the prop_1 and prop_2" implies that whenever both prop_1 and prop_2 are true, prop_0 must also be true. The word "shall" indicates that this condition must always hold. Therefore, we use the Global (G) operator to signify 'always' and an implication (->) to represent 'when'. The condition for the implication is 'prop_1 and prop_2', which is represented by 'prop_1 & prop_2'. The consequence is 'prop_0'.

The natural language phrase "The autopilot shall prop_0 when prop_1 and prop_2" implies a global implication. "When prop_1 and prop_2" sets the condition, meaning if both 'prop_1' and 'prop_2' are true. "The autopilot shall prop_0" means 'prop_0' must be true under that condition. The "shall" indicates that this condition-action relationship must always hold true, hence the 'G' (Globally) operator. Therefore, the LTL translates to 'Globally (if (prop_1 AND prop_2) then prop_0)'.

The statement "The autopilot shall prop_0 when prop_1 and prop_2" means that it must always be the case that if 'prop_1' and 'prop_2' are simultaneously true, then 'prop_0' must also be true at that same instant. The 'shall' indicates a global requirement, hence the G operator. The 'when' indicates a conditional implication.

The statement "The autopilot shall prop_0 when prop_1 and prop_2" means that it must always be the case that if both prop_1 and prop_2 are true, then prop_0 must also be true. This is a global implication.

The statement "prop_0 when prop_1 and prop_2" implies that whenever the condition "prop_1 and prop_2" holds, "prop_0" must also hold. This is best represented by a global implication, G(condition -> consequence). Thus, it translates to G((prop_1 & prop_2) -> prop_0).

The keyword 'when' generally translates to a global implication in LTL. If 'prop_1 and prop_2' (the condition) is true, then 'prop_0' (the consequence) must also be true at that same point in time. This is expressed as 'G((prop_1 & prop_2) -> prop_0)'.
